skill.name: javascript-typescript-typescript-scaffold
skill.description: Scaffold production-ready TypeScript projects for Next.js, React with Vite, Node.js APIs, libraries, and CLIs using pnpm, strict typing, tests, and common configuration.
skill.summary: Create structured TypeScript project templates with tooling, tests, and configuration guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command findings are confirmed as documentation that can lead an agent to run npm, pnpm, git, mkdir, cd, and echo commands while scaffolding projects. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or real secret material was found, but publication should warn users that commands may install packages and modify the workspace.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Executed Package Manager and Filesystem Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run npm, pnpm, git, mkdir, cd, and echo commands while creating TypeScript projects. This is consistent with the scaffold purpose, but it can install global tools, fetch packages, initialize repositories, and write files in the user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and would modify the environment if followed. They appear task-appropriate rather than malicious, so the risk is operational instead of abusive.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variable Templates Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill references .env files and provides .env.example placeholders for DATABASE_URL and JWT_SECRET. No real credentials are present, but users should replace placeholders and avoid exposing real secrets in generated projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported environment access is visible in template text and placeholder values. It does not show exfiltration or real secret harvesting, but copied placeholders could create insecure projects.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Detections Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many static external command detections point to JSON, TypeScript, project-tree, and package script examples inside Markdown fences. These are not Ruby backticks and do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections are Markdown examples and configuration snippets. They lack an execution wrapper, obfuscation, or data exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Not Evidenced
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm location is the frontmatter description line. No cryptographic primitive or hash algorithm is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains descriptive metadata, not cryptographic code. I did not find evidence for weak cryptography in the reviewed skill content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands Embedded in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell commands for installing pnpm, creating projects, initializing git, and writing ignore rules. These commands are useful for scaffolding but should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are directly present in the instructions. Their purpose is legitimate, but they can change the local development environment.

content.user_title: Scaffold TypeScript Apps With Modern Tooling
content.value_statement: TypeScript setup can be slow and inconsistent across teams. This skill provides repeatable scaffolds, tooling choices, and configuration templates for common project types.
actual_capabilities: Identifies a suitable project type for Next.js, React with Vite, Node.js APIs, libraries, or CLIs.
Produces directory structures for frontend apps, backend APIs, and reusable TypeScript libraries.
Recommends pnpm, strict TypeScript settings, ESLint, and Vitest for new projects.
Provides package scripts and dependency groups for common TypeScript project templates.
Includes guidance for .env.example files, .gitignore entries, README content, and test folders.
limitations: Package versions in the skill may need review before use in current projects.
Command examples can install packages and modify the local workspace when executed.
Generated scaffolds still need project-specific security, deployment, and authentication decisions.
The skill gives templates and guidance; it does not validate the finished application automatically.

use_cases.title: Start a Frontend Application
use_cases.description: Create a React with Vite or Next.js TypeScript project with routing, components, tests, and build scripts.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap a Typed API Service
use_cases.description: Set up an Express-based TypeScript API with routes, controllers, services, middleware, environment templates, and tests.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Reusable Package
use_cases.description: Generate a TypeScript library layout with build configuration, exports, tests, and package publishing basics.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Starter
prompt_templates.prompt: Scaffold a React with Vite TypeScript project for a small dashboard. Include folders, scripts, testing, and setup notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Next.js App Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Next.js TypeScript scaffold with app routing, shared components, API health route, Tailwind support, and Vitest testing.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Node.js API Scaffold
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Node.js TypeScript API scaffold using Express, strict typing, environment validation, route modules, services, and test organization.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Production Library Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reusable TypeScript library scaffold with exports, build configuration, test setup, release scripts, and documentation sections.

output_examples.input: I need a Vite TypeScript dashboard starter.
output: A project tree for a React with Vite app.
Recommended scripts for development, build, test, lint, and type checking.
Notes for components, hooks, test setup, and README sections.

output_examples.input: Create a typed API service scaffold.
output: A backend folder layout with routes, controllers, services, middleware, and configuration.
Suggested dependencies for Express, dotenv, Zod, TypeScript, Vitest, and ESLint.
Guidance for environment templates and health routes.

output_examples.input: Prepare a TypeScript package for reuse.
output: A library structure with source, tests, build config, and distribution folder.
Package metadata guidance for module exports, type definitions, and publish preparation.

best_practices: Review generated package versions and commands before executing them in a real workspace.
Keep strict TypeScript settings enabled unless a specific dependency requires an exception.
Replace all environment placeholders and keep real secrets outside committed files.
anti_patterns: Running scaffold commands in an existing project without checking for file conflicts.
Copying placeholder JWT secrets or database URLs into deployed environments.
Using one scaffold layout for every project type without adapting architecture needs.

faq.question: What project types does this skill support?
faq.answer: It covers Next.js apps, React with Vite apps, Node.js APIs, TypeScript libraries, and CLI-style projects.

faq.question: Does it execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides command examples. Users should review commands before allowing an agent to run them.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends Vitest setup, test folders, setup files, and test-related package scripts.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets safely?
faq.answer: It uses .env.example placeholders and .gitignore guidance. Real secrets still require user review and secure storage.

faq.question: Are the dependency versions guaranteed current?
faq.answer: No. Review dependency versions against current project requirements before installing packages.
